root.narration: Runner on first, one out. You're the left fielder. The batter hits a clean single to center field. The runner on first is aggressive and is trying to go all the way to third! Your center fielder scoops the ball and throws to third base. The throw is on line but might get past the third baseman. Where should you be right now?

choices.text: Sprint toward the area behind third base to back up the throw in case it gets past the third baseman!
Stay in left field -- that's my position and I need to be ready for the next play.
Run in to back up second base in case the batter tries to advance.

outcome-backup-great.narration: You take off sprinting the moment you see the throw going to third! You curve behind the third base area, about 20 feet behind the bag in foul territory. The throw from center field gets past the third baseman and bounces toward the dugout -- but YOU'RE THERE! You scoop it up and hold the runner at third. The batter stays at first. Your hustle just saved a run!

outcome.headline: Incredible Hustle! Backup Saves a Run!
outcome.explanation: This is one of the most under-appreciated plays in baseball. Most people don't see the left fielder sprinting behind third base because all eyes are on the throw. But that backup is CRITICAL. If that ball gets past the third baseman with nobody backing up, it rolls to the dugout and the runner scores easily. By being 15-20 feet behind the base in a direct line with the throw, you're the safety net. Great outfielders are ALWAYS moving to back up throws, even when the ball isn't near them.

keyTerms.definition: Moving to a position behind a base or fielder in case a throw gets past them. The backup fielder prevents extra bases on overthrows.
Position yourself in a straight line behind the base, in line with where the throw is coming FROM. This way the ball comes right to you if it gets through.
Even when the ball isn't hit to you, outfielders should always be moving to back up a base or another fielder. Never stand and watch.

outcome.whatToRemember: When a throw is going to a base, SOMEONE needs to be behind it. Left fielders back up third base. Right fielders back up first and second. Always be moving, even when the play isn't near you.

outcome-stay-bad.narration: You stay in right field, watching the play from 200 feet away. The throw from center field gets past the third baseman and rolls all the way to the dugout fence! The runner scores from third easily, and the batter advances to second! Nobody was there to back up the throw. Your coach turns to you: 'That's your ball to back up!'

outcome.headline: No Backup! Run Scores on Overthrow!
outcome.explanation: Standing in left field watching the play is the worst thing an outfielder can do. Even though the ball wasn't hit to you, you still had a JOB on this play -- backing up third base. At any level of baseball, throws get past fielders. That's just part of the game. The defense's job is to make sure ONE overthrow doesn't turn into a disaster. If you're backing up the throw, that overthrow only costs about 5 feet instead of letting the runner score. Outfielders should NEVER be standing still when the ball is in play.
outcome.whatToRemember: Outfielders are NEVER spectators. On every single play, you should be moving to back up a throw or a base. Never stand and watch.

outcome-second-okay.narration: You run in to back up second base. That's good hustle, but the throw is going to THIRD base, not second. The ball gets past the third baseman and nobody is there to stop it. The runner scores and the batter takes second. Your instinct to back up was right, but you went to the wrong base.

outcome.headline: Right Idea, Wrong Base!
outcome.explanation: You had the right instinct -- you need to be backing up somewhere. But you need to back up the base where the THROW is going, not just the nearest base. The center fielder's throw was going to third base, so that's where you needed to be. Think about it this way: before you start running, ask yourself 'Where is the throw going?' and then run behind THAT base. It takes practice to read the play quickly, but backing up the right base is one of the best things an outfielder can do.
outcome.whatToRemember: Always back up the base where the THROW is going. Read the play, figure out where the ball will end up, and get behind that spot.
